papergraph.title: Bio AI Agent: A Multi-Agent Artificial Intelligence System for Autonomous CAR-T Cell Therapy Development with Integrated Target Discovery, Toxicity Prediction, and Rational Molecular Design
papergraph.citation: Y. Ni et al. (2025) Bio AI Agent: A Multi-Agent Artificial Intelligence System for Autonomous CAR-T Cell Therapy Development with Integrated Target Discovery, Toxicity Prediction, and Rational Molecular Design doi:10.48550/arXiv.2511.08649
papergraph.abstract: Chimeric antigen receptor T-cell (CAR-T) therapy represents a paradigm shift in cancer treatment, yet development timelines of 8-12 years and clinical attrition rates exceeding 40-60% highlight critical inefficiencies in target selection, safety assessment, and molecular optimization. We present Bio AI Agent, a multi-agent artificial intelligence system powered by large language models that enables autonomous CAR-T development through collaborative specialized agents. The system comprises six autonomous agents: Target Selection Agent for multi-parametric antigen prioritization across>10,000 cancer-associated targets, Toxicity Prediction Agent for comprehensive safety profiling integrating tissue expression atlases and pharmacovigilance databases, Molecular Design Agent for rational CAR engineering, Patent Intelligence Agent for freedom-to-operate analysis, Clinical Translation Agent for regulatory compliance, and Decision Orchestration Agent for multi-agent coordination. Retrospective validation demonstrated autonomous identification of high-risk targets including FcRH5 (hepatotoxicity) and CD229 (off-tumor toxicity), patent infringement risks for CD38+SLAMF7 combinations, and generation of comprehensive development roadmaps. By enabling parallel processing, specialized reasoning, and autonomous decision-making superior to monolithic AI systems, Bio AI Agent addresses critical gaps in precision oncology development and has potential to accelerate translation of next-generation immunotherapies from discovery to clinic.
strengths: retrospective validation on high-risk targets and patent risks

papergraph.title: From Antigen to Atlas: A Multi-omics, Single-Cell Pipeline for Discovering Safe and Effective CAR Targets in Ovarian Cancer
papergraph.citation: Faiz Muhammad Khand et al. (2026) From Antigen to Atlas: A Multi-omics, Single-Cell Pipeline for Discovering Safe and Effective CAR Targets in Ovarian Cancer. doi:10.1016/j.critrevonc.2026.105249
papergraph.abstract: Chimeric antigen receptor (CAR) T-cell therapy has achieved remarkable success in hematologic malignancies but continues to face significant barriers in solid tumors such as ovarian cancer (OC), primarily due to antigen heterogeneity and on-target, off-tumor toxicity. Traditional discovery methods that rely on bulk tissue analyses are inadequate to overcome these challenges. This review proposes a paradigm shift toward a comprehensive, multi-omics, single-cell pipeline for the grading and validation of safe and effective CAR targets in OC. We establish a three-tiered homogeneity classification system (Grades A through C) that directly informs engineering strategies, from monotherapy for Grade A targets to combinatorial targeting for Grade B targets and deprioritization for Grade C targets. Using CLDN18.2 as a validation case, we demonstrate how this pipeline classifies targets according to homogeneity levels and prescribes specific therapeutic solutions. We detail a sequential framework that integrates bulk omics atlases, single-cell and spatial transcriptomics, glycoproteomics, and immune phenotyping to deconstruct both tumor complexity and the immunosuppressive microenvironment. These data feed into a computational prioritization funnel that scores candidate antigens based on tumor specificity, homogeneity, tumor microenvironment (TME) fitness, and essentiality. We then outline a tiered experimental validation pathway, from patient-derived organoids (PDOs) to in vivo safety models. The practical utility of this atlas-driven approach is demonstrated through a case study on claudin 18.2 (CLDN18.2), in which integrated data diagnosis informs rational engineering strategies such as combinatorial targeting and affinity tuning to mitigate limitations. Finally, we discuss reporting standards, practical feasibility, and future directions, emphasizing the transformative potential of artificial intelligence and collaborative atlases. This holistic pipeline aims to bridge the critical gap between computational discovery and clinically viable, next-generation CAR-T therapies for ovarian cancer.

papergraph.title: 116 Development of logic gated CAR-NK cells for the treatment of solid tumors
papergraph.citation: A. G. Juncà et al. (2021) 116 Development of logic gated CAR-NK cells for the treatment of solid tumors doi:10.1136/jitc-2021-sitc2021.116
papergraph.abstract: CAR-based therapies have transformed the treatment of several cancers, but this progress has not translated into solid tumors. One challenge of CAR-mediated therapies for solid tumors is the lack of specific tumor-associated antigens (TAA’s) that are only expressed on cancer cells and not on healthy cells, thereby posing a risk for on-target off-tumor toxicities. This presents a unique opportunity to use Logic Gates to expand the universe of cancer targets that may be treated with CAR-based cell therapies. CEA, a widely expressed tumor antigen, found in >90% of colorectal cancer (CRC), is also expressed in healthy gastrointestinal and lung epithelial cells. Clinical experience targeting CEA resulted in severe dose-limiting toxicities,1,2 highlighting the need for healthy tissue protection. Logic-gated gene circuits can prevent off-tumor toxicities by pairing a CEA activating-CAR (aCAR) with an inhibitory-CAR (iCAR) that recognizes a safety antigen (SA) uniquely expressed in healthy epithelial cells.We developed a bioinformatics-driven antigen paired discovery platform using single-cell transcriptomics to discover and prioritize TAA’s and pair them with SA’s that are selectively expressed on the membrane of healthy cells. TAA’s and SA’s were validated in primary cancer and healthy tissue samples using IHC. We constructed aCAR/iCAR gene circuits and tested their function in NK cells.Our bioinformatics platform identified VSIG2 to be co-expressed with CEA in healthy gastrointestinal and lung epithelial cells. IHC confirmed the expression of VSIG2 on the membrane of healthy colon (N=72 samples) and lung (N=24 samples) epithelial cells.Using our Design-Build-Test-Learn platform, we screened >250 CAR constructs targeting CEA. CAR-NK cells were generated and tested for anti-tumor activity against CRC CEA+ cells and lead candidates were selected based on NK cell performance. A single dose of CEA-CAR-NK cells had anti-tumor activity in a human CRC xenograft model, reducing tumor burden in >33% of the treated mice. We identified iCARs with different intracellular domains derived from native domains containing immunoreceptor tyrosine-based inhibitory motifs. These iCARs suppressed >50% of aCAR-mediated killing (p<0.05) and significantly reduced TNFa secretion (p<0.0005) in a SA-specific manner.We are developing Logic-Gated CAR-NK cell therapies aimed at reducing on-target off-tumor toxicities, to spare healthy cells in a SA-dependent manner. SENTI-401 will focus on targeting CEA+ CRC tumors with a NOT gate that recognizes the SA VSIG2 in the colon and lungs.Parkhurst M, et al. T cells targeting carcinoembryonic antigen can mediate regression of metastatic colorectal cancer but induce severe transient colitis. Mol Ther 2011; Mar;19(3):620–6.Thistlethwaite FC, et al. The clinical efficacy of first-generation carcinoembryonic antigen (CEACAM5)-specific CAR T cells is limited by poor persistence and transient pre-conditioning-dependent respiratory toxicity. Cancer Immunol Immunother 2017 Nov;66(11):1425–1436.

papergraph.title: Interactions Among Tumor Subtype, PPARγ Expression, and Adipose Proliferation Shape Outcomes in Breast Cancer
papergraph.citation: Shah, A. et al. (2025) Interactions Among Tumor Subtype, PPARγ Expression, and Adipose Proliferation Shape Outcomes in Breast Cancer doi:10.1101/2025.07.08.25331111
papergraph.abstract: Breast cancer progression is influenced by tumor subtype, metabolic environment, and patient factors, including menopausal status and BMI. In this study, we utilize publicly available data to investigate the prognostic relevance of PPAR{gamma} gene expression, a key regulator of lipid metabolism, and its implications across different subgroups. We also examine the proliferation of adipose tissue in patients with various tumor subtypes and phenotypic cohorts. We analyzed RNA-seq data from 1094 primary breast cancer patients in the TCGA-BRCA cohort, stratifying patients by PPAR{gamma} expression, menopausal status, and tumor receptor subtype (ER+, HER2-, TNBC) using the UCSC Xena Browser Viewer. Kaplan-Meier analysis revealed that high PPAR{gamma} expression ([≥]6.903 FPKM) was significantly associated with both improved overall and disease-specific survival, particularly in premenopausal patients. Complementing this, we analyzed PET-CT scans from 69 breast cancer patients in the ACRIN-6888 clinical trial, focusing on SUV metrics (mean, max, peak) of a cell cycle tracer, 3-deoxy-3-[18F]-fluorothymidine (18F-FLT) in visceral and subcutaneous adipose tissue at the L3/L4 level. Postmenopausal patients had lower visceral SUVmean (0.705 vs 0.776), and patients with ER+ tumors or non-TNBC tumors showed significantly lower SUVpeak and SUVmax of adipose tissue compared to their counterparts (p < 0.05), indicating metabolic/proliferative reprogramming of adipose tissue based on tumor type. Our study provides a deeper understanding of PPAR{gamma} as a therapeutic target in breast cancer through lipid metabolism pathways. The changes in adipose tissue proliferation across cohorts demonstrate the strong potential for subtype-specific breast cancer treatment.

papergraph.title: Antibody Responses to Salmonella Typhi Antigens among Typhoid Recovered Individuals in an Endemic Region
papergraph.citation: Athavale, A. et al. (2025) Antibody Responses to Salmonella Typhi Antigens among Typhoid Recovered Individuals in an Endemic Region doi:10.1101/2025.07.04.663140
papergraph.abstract:
BackgroundTyphoid fever remains a major public health concern in endemic regions, yet the nature of antibody responses to individual Salmonella Typhi antigens and their potential role in modulating cellular immune functions are not well understood. This gap also applies to the long-term immune response after the vaccination against the disease.

ObjectivesTo evaluate the humoral and cellular immune response against the Salmonella Typhi antigens among the Typhoid recovered individuals.

MethodsELISA was used to measure antibody responses against five S. Typhi antigens in plasma samples from typhoid-recovered individuals and healthy participants. Vi polysaccharide-specific plasma antibodies were tested for their ability to activate NK cells isolated from healthy donors. Flow cytometry-based assays were performed to analyze T cell and B cell profiles in the peripheral circulation of these individuals.

ResultsPolysaccharide antigens mounted better IgA responses among typhoid-recovered individuals compared with healthy participants. Anti-S. Typhi IgM was significantly elevated in the typhoid group for all five antigens (p<0.05), while no significant differences were observed in IgG responses. NK cell activation assays showed comparable levels of activation markers of the NK cells isolated from healthy individuals and treated with the plasma of Typhoid recovered individuals. Analysis of B cell subsets revealed largely comparable distributions between the two groups; however, transitional B cells were elevated among the Typhoid recovered individuals. HlyE-specific memory B cells were detectable in both the groups. CD4 T cell subsets were similar between typhoid-recovered and healthy individuals, whereas differences were observed in CD8 T cell populations, with higher TEMRA cells among recovered individuals and relatively higher central memory CD8 T cells in healthy participants.
papergraph.conclusion: Polysaccharide antigens elicited significantly better IgA response in recovered typhoid participants, possibly reflecting mucosal immune priming from the gut. Persistent IgM antibodies after recovery suggest durable IgM responses following typhoid infection. Similar IgG levels between groups and the presence of antigen-specific memory cells in healthy individuals highlight the influence of repeated exposure in endemic settings. Elevated transitional B cells suggest the reinforcement of the B cells to the peripheral circulation during the disease which remained in the circulation of these individuals. However, elevated TEMRA population of the CD8 T cells has been observed in several other infectious diseases including this study. These findings demonstrate the complexity of humoral and cellular immune responses to S. Typhi in endemic regions.

papergraph.title: Circumcision for HIV prevention in men who have sex with men: an updated meta-analysis
papergraph.citation: Davis, S. et al. (2025) Circumcision for HIV prevention in men who have sex with men: an updated meta-analysis doi:10.1101/2025.07.02.25330772
papergraph.abstract:
BackgroundWhile circumcision reduces HIV risk for men who have sex with women, its value for men who have sex with men (MSM) remains unclear. Any protection would be for primarily-insertive MSM (PI-MSM) and could have substantial impact given high incidence. Prior meta-analyses were ambiguous due to inconsistent, highly-confounded data, leaving the total weight of evidence unclear even after a small 2024 trial in PI-MSM showed protection.

SettingNo geographic or other setting restrictions were used.

MethodsWe performed an updated literature search for data on the association of male circumcision with HIV among MSM. We conducted descriptive analyses and a random-effects meta-analysis to assess the main association among PI-MSM. To isolate the protective effect of circumcision from potential confounding factors, we also performed a confounder-adjusted meta-analysis, comparing the effect of circumcision on HIV status among primarily-insertive MSM versus among other (non-primarily-insertive) MSM in studies with stratified results.

ResultsForty-nine studies were included in the descriptive analysis, 13 in the unadjusted meta-analysis, and 10 in the confounder-adjusted meta-analysis. In the unadjusted meta-analysis, circumcision was associated with a lower risk for HIV among PI-MSM (odds ratio (OR) = 0.57, 95% CI: 0.33-0.98); publication bias was present. In the confounder-adjusted analysis, the additional effect of circumcision in the PI-MSM group - a ratio of ORs - was 0.53 (95% CI: 0.34- 0.83), indicating a lower bound on the protective effect of circumcision among PI-MSM adjusting for confounding factors. Publication bias was not present.

ConclusionsAmong PI-MSM, circumcision was protective against HIV. Findings support male circumcision as an effective HIV prevention method for PI-MSM, alongside established combination prevention methods including pre-exposure prophylaxis.
papergraph.conclusion: Among PI-MSM, circumcision was protective against HIV. Findings support male circumcision as an effective HIV prevention method for PI-MSM, alongside established combination prevention methods including pre-exposure prophylaxis.

papergraph.title: Navigating parasite antigen genetic diversity in the design of Plasmodium vivax serological exposure markers for malaria
papergraph.citation: Bareng, P. et al. (2025) Navigating parasite antigen genetic diversity in the design of Plasmodium vivax serological exposure markers for malaria doi:10.1101/2025.07.07.663616
papergraph.abstract:
BackgroundPlasmodium vivax poses a major obstacle to malaria elimination because it can lie dormant in the liver for weeks or months before reactivating and causing a relapse of infection. These dormant forms (hypnozoites) cannot be detected using standard diagnostics, but recent P. vivax exposure and by proxy, hypnozoite carriage, can be inferred using antibody-based tests (serological markers). In this study, we examined how genetic variation in P. vivax affects the utility of these antibody markers, and whether redesigned antigens could improve performance.
MethodsWe analysed global P. vivax genetic data to assess variation in leading serological markers. Based on this, we produced new antigen versions (haplotypes) that better reflect global sequence diversity, compared to the commonly used reference strain (Sal-1). Antibody responses against these new constructs were then tested using samples from well-characterised cohorts in Brazil and Thailand. Antibody levels were assessed in relation to how recently participants had a qPCR-detectable blood-stage P. vivax infection. We compared the ability of the haplotypes and reference constructs to correctly identify individuals infected within the prior 9-months.
FindingsExtensive genetic diversity was identified in two P. vivax antigens, DBPII and MSP5. Several antigens had large numbers of circulating haplotypes globally, with the percentage with similar sequence identity to the reference Sal-1 ranging from 0.4% (MSP5) to 99% (S16). Two antigens exhibited strong differences in immunogenicity by region and construct (RBP2a and DBPII). However, for most proteins (5 out of 8), these differences had little impact on the accuracy of identifying recent exposure. In cases where performance was affected (e.g. RBP2a), this could be overcome by adding multiple antigens into the classification model.
InterpretationEven highly diverse antigens can be effective serological exposure markers. Our findings highlight the importance of testing the impact of genetic diversity when designing serological tests and suggest practical strategies, such as using a mix of antigens, to ensure consistent performance across regions.

papergraph.title: Health Disparity and Geospatial Analysis among Cancer Patients in the US/Mexico Border Region of Imperial Valley
papergraph.citation: Fontelera, K. et al. (2025) Health Disparity and Geospatial Analysis among Cancer Patients in the US/Mexico Border Region of Imperial Valley doi:10.1101/2025.07.07.25331030
papergraph.abstract: PurposeDisparities in access to treatment and quality of care among cancer patients is an important issue that influences disease outcomes in minority populations. Although past research regarding the association between travel and health outcomes of cancer patients exists, studies in rural regions such as the US-Mexico border are limited. The purpose of this study is to understand the spatial patterns and travel distance association on the health outcomes of various cancers in underserved rural regions in Southern California (Imperial Valley). MethodsPatients were identified from the intake assessment data form for new patients in a community-based cancer organization (CBO) between 2006 and 2022. Analysis was conducted using ArcGIS Pro to visualize spatial patterns of cancers and to identify the relationship between travel distance and mortality. The relationship between ethnicity and cancer deaths were also investigated. Kernel Density Estimation (KDE) plots were used to visualize spatial patterns of certain groups. ResultsThere is a significant association between ethnicity and cancer deaths. The odds of death are slightly higher among Hispanics/Latinos. As hospital distance increased, the odds of mortality decreased. There were no significant associations between travel distances and any of the stages of diagnosis and death. There were three types of spatial distribution patterns of cancers respective of the overall dataset.
papergraph.conclusion: Our study found three types of spatial distribution patterns of various cancers in Imperial Valley. Cancer patient mortality did not have statistical significance associations with travel distance to treatment. However, further investigation and better tools may yield clearer results regarding its correlation.

papergraph.title: Revolutionizing Cancer Treatment: Recent Advances in Immunotherapy
papergraph.citation: (2024) Revolutionizing Cancer Treatment: Recent Advances in Immunotherapy doi:10.3390/biomedicines12092158
papergraph.abstract: Cancer immunotherapy has emerged as a transformative approach in oncology, utilizing the body’s immune system to specifically target and destroy malignant cells. This review explores the scope and impact of various immunotherapeutic strategies, including monoclonal antibodies, chimeric antigen receptor (CAR)-T cell therapy, checkpoint inhibitors, cytokine therapy, and therapeutic vaccines. Monoclonal antibodies, such as Rituximab and Trastuzumab, have revolutionized treatment paradigms for lymphoma and breast cancer by offering targeted interventions that reduce off-target effects. CAR-T cell therapy presents a potentially curative option for refractory hematologic malignancies, although challenges remain in effectively treating solid tumors. Checkpoint inhibitors have redefined the management of cancers like melanoma and lung cancer; however, managing immune-related adverse events and ensuring durable responses are critical areas of focus. Cytokine therapy continues to play a vital role in modulating the immune response, with advancements in cytokine engineering improving specificity and reducing systemic toxicity. Therapeutic vaccines, particularly mRNA-based vaccines, represent a frontier in personalized cancer treatment, aiming to generate robust, long-lasting immune responses against tumor-specific antigens. Despite these advancements, the field faces significant challenges, including immune resistance, tumor heterogeneity, and the immunosuppressive tumor microenvironment. Future research should address these obstacles through emerging technologies, such as next-generation antibodies, Clustered Regularly Interspaced Short Palindromic Repeat (CRISPR)-based gene editing, and AI-driven drug discovery. By integrating these novel approaches, cancer immunotherapy holds the promise of offering more durable, less toxic, and highly personalized treatment options, ultimately improving patient outcomes and survival rates.

papergraph.title: Taurine is a Natural Host Cytoadhesion Inhibitor in Asymptomatic Malaria Cases
papergraph.citation: Diffendall, G. et al. (2025) Taurine is a Natural Host Cytoadhesion Inhibitor in Asymptomatic Malaria Cases doi:10.1101/2025.07.06.663375
papergraph.abstract: The prolonged dry season in malaria-endemic regions of sub-Saharan Africa can be divided into periods of high and low transmission. The majority of symptomatic malaria cases are restricted to the short high transmission period that follows the rainy season. Shortly after, persistent asymptomatic malaria cases are more prevalent throughout the prolonged low transmission period. It is still unclear whether host metabolic alterations play a role in asymptomatic infections during seasonal malaria. In this study, we analyzed the blood plasma metabolome (n=199) of individuals in The Gambia, West Africa, capturing data from both high and low malaria transmission periods. Plasma samples from individuals (n=16) were collected monthly throughout the low transmission season, enabling a longitudinal analysis of metabolic alternations over six months. Our findings reveal that significant changes in host plasma metabolite composition are associated with seasonality and malaria pathogenicity. Notably, we observed elevated levels of taurine in asymptomatic malaria infections, especially during periods of low transmission. In vitro, this naturally occurring host molecule inhibits the cytoadhesion of malaria-infected red blood cells (iRBCs), which is key to malaria disease severity and mortality. Exogenous taurine can significantly reduce or reverse binding of iRBCs to the common adhesion receptor CD36 and the endothelial protein C receptor (EPCR), the later being associated with cerebral malaria. This study uncovers a mechanism by which elevated taurine plasma levels in asymptomatic infections could reduce cytoadhesion and lead to increased splenic clearance, thereby strengthening host resistance to symptomatic infections. In the absence of health strategies targeting dry season parasite reservoirs, our findings highlight taurine as a potential prophylactic or therapeutic agent to reduce symptomatic malaria in sub-Saharan Africa.

papergraph.title: Not Just Noise: Impaired Oscillatory Entrainment Reflects Reduced Temporal Flexibility in Autism
papergraph.citation: Beker, S. et al. (2025) Not Just Noise: Impaired Oscillatory Entrainment Reflects Reduced Temporal Flexibility in Autism doi:10.1101/2025.07.02.662876
papergraph.abstract: Rhythmic patterns in the environment enhance neural activity, perception, and action. However, natural rhythms are often imprecise, requiring flexible adaptation. In autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD), characterized by cognitive rigidity and atypical use of prior information - favoring immediate sensory input over predictive cues - entrainment to temporally variable input may be reduced at both neural and behavioral levels, though the neural mechanisms remain unclear. Here, we recorded high-density EEG and behavior in adults with ASD (n=20) and neurotypical (NT) controls (n=21) during a visual detection task with four rhythmic structures, parametrically varied from an isochronous fully regular rhythm, to a highly irregular one. Spectral analysis and temporal response function (TRF) models revealed significantly reduced modulation by temporal regularity in ASD, particularly in mildly jittered stimulation streams. Additionally, the coupling between phases of neural oscillations and behavior was diminished in ASD under the jittered conditions, suggesting reduced functional relevance of neural synchronization. Residual spectral power post-stimulation showed lower oscillatory entrainment in ASD, ruling out simple evoked-response explanations. Notably, the degree of neural modulation by temporal regularity was correlated with IQ within the ASD group, suggesting a link between temporal flexibility and individual cognitive profiles. These findings highlight impaired neural entrainment and reduced behavioral modulation by temporal structure in ASD, offering insight into inflexible responses to uncertain, volatile sensory environments.

papergraph.title: Preventing premature deaths through polygenic risk scores
papergraph.citation: Chuong, M. et al. (2025) Preventing premature deaths through polygenic risk scores doi:10.1101/2024.12.26.24319670
papergraph.abstract:
BackgroundPolygenic risk scores (PRS) have demonstrated predictive validity across a range of cohorts and diseases, but quantifying their clinical utility remains a challenge. As PRS can be derived from a single biological sample and remain stable throughout life, we explore the potential of PRS to optimize existing screening programs.

MethodsVia an integrated modelling approach, we quantified the potential clinical benefits arising from a knowledge of PRS across seven diseases with existing screening programs (abdominal aortic aneurysm, breast cancer, colorectal cancer, coronary artery disease, hypertension, prostate cancer, and type 2 diabetes). We identified individuals at high genetic risk (PRS OR>2) and very high genetic risk (PRS OR>3) and estimated the optimal screening ages for these genetically high risk individuals, based on the equivalent risk to population-level risk at recommended screening ages. We then leveraged published data on differential mortality and other outcomes, with and without screening-based interventions, to assess the potential benefits of tailoring screening age based on genetic risk. We also estimated the case enrichment ratio, which is a ratio of the percentage of cases in the high PRS risk group and in the total population.

FindingsVery high risk individuals reach the risk level associated with usual starting screening age on average 10.8 years earlier, high risk individuals 8.9 years earlier and reduced risk individuals (OR<0.5) 16.8 years later. During this time, case enrichment in the high risk group is between 1.7 and 3.0 depending on disease. Across all seven diseases, appropriate interventions following PRS-guided screening could reduce premature deaths in high risk individuals by 23.3%.

ConclusionKnowledge of genetic risk, measured using PRS, has the potential to deliver substantial public health benefits when aggregated across conditions, and could reduce premature mortality by tailoring existing screening programs.
papergraph.conclusion: Knowledge of genetic risk, measured using PRS, has the potential to deliver substantial public health benefits when aggregated across conditions, and could reduce premature mortality by tailoring existing screening programs.

keynotes.title: Bio AI Agent: A Multi-Agent Artificial Intelligence System for Autonomous CAR-T Cell Therapy Development with Integrated Target Discovery, Toxicity Prediction, and Rational Molecular Design

keynote.title: Bio AI Agent: A Multi-Agent Artificial Intelligence System for Autonomous CAR-T Cell Therapy Development with Integrated Target Discovery, Toxicity Prediction, and Rational Molecular Design
keynote.core_problem: Excessively long CAR-T development timelines (8–12 years) and high clinical attrition rates (40–60%), driven by inefficiencies in target selection, safety assessment, and molecular optimization.
contributions: A multi-agent AI system (Bio AI Agent) composed of six specialized, collaborative LLM-based agents for end-to-end CAR-T development.
Autonomous integration of target discovery, toxicity prediction, and rational molecular design within a single framework.
Retrospective validation showing identification of high-risk targets (e.g., FcRH5, CD229), patent infringement risks (e.g., CD38+SLAMF7), and generation of development roadmaps.
methods: Multi-agent architecture powered by large language models.

Specialized agent roles: Target Selection Agent (multi-parametric antigen prioritization across >10,000 cancer-associated targets), Toxicity Prediction Agent (integration of tissue expression atlases and pharmacovigilance databases), Molecular Design Agent (rational CAR engineering), Patent Intellig...[truncated]
Parallel processing and autonomous decision-making via agent collaboration.
experiments_or_evidence: Retrospective validation demonstrating autonomous identification of hepatotoxicity risk for FcRH5 and off-tumor toxicity for CD229.
Retrospective detection of patent infringement risk for CD38+SLAMF7 combinations.
Generation of comprehensive CAR-T development roadmaps.

gap_signals.text: Monolithic AI systems are insufficient for autonomous, parallel, and specialized reasoning required in CAR-T development.
Development timelines of 8–12 years and clinical attrition rates exceeding 40–60% highlight critical inefficiencies in target selection, safety assessment, and molecular optimization.

datasets_or_materials: Tissue expression atlases
Pharmacovigilance databases
Patent databases (implied by Patent Intelligence Agent)
Cancer-associated target database (>10,000 targets)

important_claims.claim: Bio AI Agent enables autonomous CAR-T development through collaborative specialized agents.
important_claims.evidence: Retrospective validation demonstrated autonomous identification of high-risk targets (FcRH5, CD229), patent infringement risks (CD38+SLAMF7), and generation of comprehensive development roadmaps.

important_claims.claim: Bio AI Agent supports parallel processing, specialized reasoning, and autonomous decision-making superior to monolithic AI systems.
important_claims.evidence: System comprises six autonomous, functionally specialized agents coordinated by a Decision Orchestration Agent; described as addressing gaps that monolithic systems cannot.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: High — provides a modular, agent-based architecture template for integrating heterogeneous biomedical data sources and domain-specific reasoning in complex therapeutic development pipelines; agents can be independently adapted or extended.

keynotes.title: From Antigen to Atlas: A Multi-omics, Single-Cell Pipeline for Discovering Safe and Effective CAR Targets in Ovarian Cancer

keynote.title: From Antigen to Atlas: A Multi-omics, Single-Cell Pipeline for Discovering Safe and Effective CAR Targets in Ovarian Cancer
keynote.core_problem: Antigen heterogeneity and on-target, off-tumor toxicity limit CAR T-cell therapy efficacy in ovarian cancer; bulk tissue–based target discovery is inadequate.
contributions: Proposes a three-tiered homogeneity classification system (Grades A–C) that directly informs CAR engineering strategies (e.g., monotherapy vs. combinatorial targeting)
Introduces a sequential, multi-omics, single-cell pipeline integrating bulk omics atlases, single-cell/spatial transcriptomics, glycoproteomics, and immune phenotyping
Defines a computational prioritization funnel scoring antigens on tumor specificity, homogeneity, TME fitness, and essentiality
Outlines a tiered experimental validation pathway (patient-derived organoids → in vivo safety models)
Demonstrates practical utility via CLDN18.2 case study, linking integrated data diagnosis to rational engineering (e.g., affinity tuning, combinatorial targeting)
Discusses reporting standards, feasibility, and future directions including AI and collaborative atlases
methods: Three-tiered homogeneity classification (Grades A–C)
Integration of bulk omics atlases
Single-cell and spatial transcriptomics
Glycoproteomics
Immune phenotyping
Computational prioritization funnel (scoring for tumor specificity, homogeneity, TME fitness, essentiality)
Tiered experimental validation: patient-derived organoids (PDOs), in vivo safety models
experiments_or_evidence: Case study validation using CLDN18.2 to demonstrate pipeline application and therapeutic prescription
Integrated data diagnosis informing rational engineering decisions (e.g., combinatorial targeting, affinity tuning)

gap_signals.text: No existing pipeline holistically integrates multi-omics, single-cell, spatial, and glycoproteomic layers with computational scoring and tiered experimental validation for CAR target discovery in ovarian cancer.
Traditional discovery methods relying on bulk tissue analyses are inadequate to overcome antigen heterogeneity and on-target, off-tumor toxicity in solid tumors.

important_claims.claim: The three-tiered homogeneity classification (Grades A–C) directly informs CAR engineering strategies.
important_claims.evidence: Demonstrated via CLDN18.2 case study where classification prescribed combinatorial targeting and affinity tuning.

important_claims.claim: A multi-omics, single-cell pipeline bridges the gap between computational discovery and clinically viable CAR-T therapies.
important_claims.evidence: Pipeline integrates bulk atlases, single-cell/spatial transcriptomics, glycoproteomics, immune phenotyping, computational scoring, and tiered experimental validation — illustrated through CLDN18.2 use case.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: Provides a reusable, modular framework for CAR target discovery in other solid tumors; establishes standardized classification, scoring criteria, and validation tiers applicable beyond ovarian cancer.

keynotes.title: 116 Development of logic gated CAR-NK cells for the treatment of solid tumors

keynote.title: Development of logic gated CAR-NK cells for the treatment of solid tumors
keynote.core_problem: On-target off-tumor toxicity in CAR-based therapies for solid tumors due to lack of truly tumor-specific antigens, exemplified by CEA expression on both colorectal cancer and healthy gastrointestinal/lung epithelial cells.
contributions: Bioinformatics-driven antigen-pair discovery platform using single-cell transcriptomics to identify tumor-associated antigens (TAA) and safety antigens (SA) with complementary expression patterns
Identification and validation of VSIG2 as a membrane-expressed safety antigen co-localized with CEA in healthy colon and lung epithelium
Design and functional validation of NOT-gated aCAR/iCAR logic circuits in NK cells, where iCAR recognizes VSIG2 to inhibit CEA-directed killing
Preclinical demonstration of CEA-CAR-NK anti-tumor activity in human CRC xenograft models
Development of SENTI-401 — a clinical-stage logic-gated CAR-NK therapy targeting CEA+ CRC with VSIG2-based NOT gate
methods: Single-cell transcriptomics analysis for TAA/SA pair discovery
Immunohistochemistry (IHC) validation of antigen expression in primary human tissue samples (N=72 colon, N=24 lung)
Design-Build-Test-Learn iterative screening of >250 CEA-targeting CAR constructs
Engineering of activating CAR (aCAR) and inhibitory CAR (iCAR) with immunoreceptor tyrosine-based inhibitory motifs (ITIMs) in NK cells
In vitro cytotoxicity and cytokine secretion assays (TNFα) to quantify logic-gate function
Human CRC xenograft mouse model for in vivo efficacy testing
experiments_or_evidence: IHC confirmed VSIG2 membrane expression in healthy colon (N=72) and lung (N=24) epithelial cells
iCARs suppressed >50% of aCAR-mediated killing (p<0.05) and significantly reduced TNFα secretion (p<0.0005) in SA-dependent manner
Single dose of CEA-CAR-NK reduced tumor burden in >33% of treated mice in human CRC xenograft model
Screening of >250 CAR constructs identified lead candidates based on NK cell performance
assumptions: VSIG2 expression is sufficiently exclusive to healthy epithelium and absent or negligible on CRC tumor cells
NK cells can stably express and functionally integrate dual aCAR/iCAR circuits with appropriate signaling kinetics
The spatial co-expression pattern of CEA and VSIG2 in tissue enables effective logic-gated discrimination in vivo
Inhibitory signaling via ITIM-containing iCAR domains is sufficient to dominantly suppress aCAR activation in physiological contexts
limitations: No data reported on VSIG2 expression status in CRC tumor samples (e.g., downregulation, heterogeneity, or loss)
No quantification of off-tumor toxicity reduction in vivo (e.g., colitis or lung toxicity metrics)
Limited characterization of NK cell persistence, trafficking, or exhaustion post-infusion
Xenograft model does not recapitulate human immune microenvironment or VSIG2+ healthy tissue architecture
No comparison to alternative gating strategies (e.g., AND, OR) or other SA candidates

gap_signals.text: Limited characterization of NK cell persistence, trafficking, or exhaustion post-infusion
No comparison to alternative gating strategies (e.g., AND, OR) or other SA candidates
No data reported on VSIG2 expression status in CRC tumor samples (e.g., downregulation, heterogeneity, or loss)
No quantification of off-tumor toxicity reduction in vivo (e.g., colitis or lung toxicity metrics)
Xenograft model does not recapitulate human immune microenvironment or VSIG2+ healthy tissue architecture
Prior CAR designs lacked integrated, antigen-selective inhibition; this work introduces a bioinformatics-guided paired-antigen logic-gating framework.
Most preclinical CAR studies use T cells; this adapts logic gates to NK cells — a less explored but potentially safer effector platform.
Clinical failure of CEA-targeted CAR-T due to colitis and respiratory toxicity highlights unmet need for healthy-tissue protection mechanisms.

datasets_or_materials: Single-cell transcriptomic datasets (source unspecified)
Primary human colon tissue samples (N=72)
Primary human lung tissue samples (N=24)
Human colorectal cancer (CRC) cell lines (CEA+)
Human CRC xenograft mouse model

important_claims.claim: VSIG2 is selectively expressed on the membrane of healthy gastrointestinal and lung epithelial cells and co-localizes with CEA.
important_claims.evidence: IHC validation in N=72 colon and N=24 lung primary tissue samples.

important_claims.claim: iCARs with native ITIM domains suppress >50% of aCAR-mediated killing and significantly reduce TNFα secretion in a safety-antigen–dependent manner.
important_claims.evidence: In vitro functional assays with statistical significance (p<0.05 for killing, p<0.0005 for TNFα).

important_claims.claim: A single dose of CEA-CAR-NK cells reduces tumor burden in >33% of mice in a human CRC xenograft model.
important_claims.evidence: In vivo xenograft efficacy experiment.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: Provides a reproducible bioinformatics + IHC pipeline for TAA/SA pairing; establishes NK-cell-compatible logic-gate design principles; identifies VSIG2 as a candidate safety antigen for GI/lung-protected targeting; offers modular CAR construct screening framework applicable beyond CEA.

keynotes.title: Interactions Among Tumor Subtype, PPARγ Expression, and Adipose Proliferation Shape Outcomes in Breast Cancer

keynote.title: Interactions Among Tumor Subtype, PPARγ Expression, and Adipose Proliferation Shape Outcomes in Breast Cancer
keynote.core_problem: Understanding how tumor subtype, PPARγ expression, and adipose tissue proliferation jointly influence breast cancer outcomes, particularly survival and metabolic reprogramming.
contributions: Demonstrated prognostic relevance of PPARγ expression (≥6.903 FPKM) for improved overall and disease-specific survival, especially in premenopausal patients.
Identified tumor subtype–associated differences in adipose tissue metabolic activity (via 18F-FLT PET-CT SUV metrics), showing lower proliferation in ER+ and non-TNBC tumors.
Integrated genomic (RNA-seq) and functional imaging (PET-CT) data to link lipid metabolism regulation with clinical phenotype and survival.
methods: Kaplan-Meier survival analysis stratified by PPARγ expression, menopausal status, and receptor subtype (ER+, HER2-, TNBC).
RNA-seq data analysis (FPKM quantification) from TCGA-BRCA cohort (n=1094).
Quantitative analysis of 18F-FLT PET-CT SUV metrics (mean, max, peak) in visceral and subcutaneous adipose tissue at L3/L4 level.
Cohort stratification using UCSC Xena Browser Viewer.
experiments_or_evidence: Kaplan-Meier analysis showing significant association between high PPARγ expression and improved survival in premenopausal patients.
Comparative SUV analysis revealing lower visceral SUVmean in postmenopausal patients (0.705 vs 0.776).
Significantly lower SUVpeak and SUVmax in adipose tissue of ER+ and non-TNBC patients vs counterparts (p < 0.05).
assumptions: PPARγ expression level (FPKM ≥6.903) is a biologically and clinically meaningful threshold.
18F-FLT uptake in adipose tissue reliably reflects proliferative activity.
Menopausal status and receptor subtype are sufficient proxies for underlying metabolic and hormonal context.

gap_signals.text: Lack of direct measurement of adipose cellularity, lipolysis, or PPARγ protein activity—only mRNA expression and indirect metabolic imaging used.
PET-CT cohort (n=69) is substantially smaller and not matched to the TCGA cohort, limiting integrated inference.
No mechanistic validation (e.g., knockdown/overexpression experiments) linking PPARγ expression to adipose proliferation or tumor behavior.

datasets_or_materials: TCGA-BRCA RNA-seq dataset (n=1094)
ACRIN-6888 clinical trial PET-CT dataset (n=69)

important_claims.claim: High PPARγ expression (≥6.903 FPKM) is significantly associated with improved overall and disease-specific survival, especially in premenopausal patients.
important_claims.evidence: Kaplan-Meier analysis on TCGA-BRCA cohort.

important_claims.claim: ER+ and non-TNBC tumors are associated with lower 18F-FLT SUVpeak and SUVmax in adipose tissue compared to HER2+ or TNBC tumors.
important_claims.evidence: Comparative SUV analysis in ACRIN-6888 PET-CT cohort (p < 0.05).

important_claims.claim: Adipose tissue exhibits metabolic/proliferative reprogramming dependent on tumor subtype.
important_claims.evidence: Differential 18F-FLT uptake across receptor-defined subgroups in PET-CT data.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: Provides reusable analytical framework for integrating transcriptomic and functional imaging data in oncology; identifies PPARγ and adipose SUV metrics as candidate biomarkers for subtype-stratified prognosis and therapy development.

keynotes.title: Antibody Responses to Salmonella Typhi Antigens among Typhoid Recovered Individuals in an Endemic Region

keynote.title: Antibody Responses to Salmonella Typhi Antigens among Typhoid Recovered Individuals in an Endemic Region
keynote.core_problem: The nature of antibody responses to individual Salmonella Typhi antigens—and their role in modulating cellular immune functions—is poorly understood, especially in endemic regions and regarding long-term immunity post-infection or vaccination.
contributions: Characterized humoral (IgA, IgM, IgG) responses to five S. Typhi antigens in typhoid-recovered vs. healthy individuals.
Assessed functional impact of Vi polysaccharide-specific antibodies on NK cell activation.
Defined alterations in B cell subsets (e.g., elevated transitional B cells) and CD8 T cell differentiation (elevated TEMRA) post-typhoid recovery.
Provided evidence of persistent IgM and antigen-specific memory B cells (e.g., HlyE-specific) in both groups.
methods: ELISA to measure antigen-specific IgA, IgM, and IgG against five S. Typhi antigens.
NK cell activation assays using plasma from recovered individuals and NK cells isolated from healthy donors.
Flow cytometry-based immunophenotyping of peripheral B cell and T cell subsets.
experiments_or_evidence: Significantly elevated anti-S. Typhi IgM in recovered individuals for all five antigens (p<0.05).
Better IgA responses to polysaccharide antigens in recovered individuals vs. healthy controls.
No significant differences in IgG responses between groups.
Comparable NK cell activation markers when treated with Vi-specific plasma.
Elevated transitional B cells in recovered individuals; detectable HlyE-specific memory B cells in both groups.
Similar CD4 T cell subsets; elevated CD8 TEMRA cells in recovered individuals, higher central memory CD8 T cells in healthy participants.
assumptions: Plasma-mediated NK cell activation reflects in vivo functional relevance.
Peripheral blood immunophenotyping adequately represents systemic immune status post-typhoid.
Presence of antigen-specific memory B cells implies functional immunological memory.
limitations: Lack of longitudinal sampling to assess durability of observed immune changes.
Use of plasma from recovered individuals to stimulate NK cells from healthy donors limits assessment of autologous interactions.
No functional assays for T cell antigen specificity or cytokine production.
Small sample size not specified; statistical power for subset analyses unclear.
No comparison to vaccinated individuals—gap remains for vaccine-induced immunity.

gap_signals.text: Small sample size not specified; statistical power for subset analyses unclear.
Lack of longitudinal sampling to assess durability of observed immune changes.
No comparison to vaccinated individuals—gap remains for vaccine-induced immunity.
No functional assays for T cell antigen specificity or cytokine production.
Use of plasma from recovered individuals to stimulate NK cells from healthy donors limits assessment of autologous interactions.
Nature of antibody responses to individual S. Typhi antigens and their role in modulating cellular immune functions is not well understood.
This gap also applies to the long-term immune response after vaccination against the disease.

datasets_or_materials: Plasma samples from typhoid-recovered individuals and healthy participants.
NK cells isolated from healthy donors.
Peripheral blood mononuclear cells (PBMCs) for flow cytometry.

important_claims.claim: Polysaccharide antigens mounted better IgA responses among typhoid-recovered individuals compared with healthy participants.
important_claims.evidence: Reported in Results section; interpreted as possibly reflecting mucosal immune priming from the gut.

important_claims.claim: Anti-S. Typhi IgM was significantly elevated in the typhoid group for all five antigens (p<0.05).
important_claims.evidence: Explicitly stated in Results.

important_claims.claim: Transitional B cells were elevated among typhoid-recovered individuals.
important_claims.evidence: Reported in Results; interpreted as reinforcement of B cells to peripheral circulation during disease that persisted post-recovery.

important_claims.claim: Elevated CD8 TEMRA cells were observed in typhoid-recovered individuals.
important_claims.evidence: Reported in Results; noted as consistent with patterns seen in other infectious diseases.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: Provides benchmark immune phenotypes (B/T subset distributions, Ig isotype profiles, NK functional readouts) for S. Typhi infection in endemic settings; enables cross-study comparison with vaccine trials or other enteric infections; highlights candidate correlates (e.g., IgM persistence, transitional B cells) for further mechanistic study.

keynotes.title: Circumcision for HIV prevention in men who have sex with men: an updated meta-analysis

keynote.title: Circumcision for HIV prevention in men who have sex with men: an updated meta-analysis
keynote.core_problem: Uncertainty about whether male circumcision reduces HIV risk among men who have sex with men (MSM), particularly primarily-insertive MSM (PI-MSM), due to inconsistent and highly confounded prior evidence.
contributions: Updated meta-analysis incorporating new evidence including a 2024 trial in PI-MSM
First confounder-adjusted meta-analysis comparing circumcision effect specifically within PI-MSM versus other MSM using stratified study data
Quantification of lower bound of protective effect of circumcision among PI-MSM after adjusting for confounding (ratio of ORs = 0.53)
methods: Updated literature search for studies on male circumcision and HIV among MSM
Descriptive analysis across 49 studies
Random-effects meta-analysis (unadjusted) on 13 studies
Confounder-adjusted meta-analysis (ratio of odds ratios) on 10 studies with stratified PI-MSM vs. non-PI-MSM results
experiments_or_evidence: Unadjusted meta-analysis: OR = 0.57 (95% CI: 0.33–0.98) for circumcision and HIV risk among PI-MSM
Confounder-adjusted analysis: ratio of ORs = 0.53 (95% CI: 0.34–0.83), indicating protective effect independent of measured confounders
Assessment of publication bias: present in unadjusted analysis; absent in confounder-adjusted analysis
assumptions: Studies reporting stratified results by insertive role provide sufficient adjustment for key confounders when comparing PI-MSM vs. non-PI-MSM within the same study
Random-effects model appropriately accounts for heterogeneity across observational and trial data
limitations: Publication bias detected in unadjusted meta-analysis
Only 10 studies contributed to confounder-adjusted analysis, limiting precision and generalizability
Reliance on observational data with potential residual confounding despite stratified design
No geographic or setting restrictions, but heterogeneity across settings not quantified or controlled

Only 10 studies contributed to confounder-adjusted analysis, limiting precision and generalizability
Publication bias detected in unadjusted meta-analysis
Reliance on observational data with potential residual confounding despite stratified design
Prior meta-analyses were ambiguous due to inconsistent, highly-confounded data.
Lack of confounder-adjusted estimation specific to PI-MSM before this analysis.
Value of circumcision for MSM remained unclear despite evidence in men who have sex with women.

important_claims.claim: Among primarily-insertive MSM, circumcision was protective against HIV.
important_claims.evidence: Confounder-adjusted ratio of ORs = 0.53 (95% CI: 0.34–0.83); unadjusted OR = 0.57 (95% CI: 0.33–0.98)

important_claims.claim: Findings support male circumcision as an effective HIV prevention method for PI-MSM alongside combination prevention (e.g., PrEP).
important_claims.evidence: Statistically significant protective association after confounder adjustment; absence of publication bias in that analysis

keynotes.title: Navigating parasite antigen genetic diversity in the design of Plasmodium vivax serological exposure markers for malaria

keynote.title: Navigating parasite antigen genetic diversity in the design of Plasmodium vivax serological exposure markers for malaria
keynote.core_problem: Genetic diversity in Plasmodium vivax antigens undermines the reliability of serological exposure markers across geographic regions, hindering accurate inference of recent infection and hypnozoite carriage.
contributions: Quantified global genetic diversity across 8 P. vivax serological marker antigens (e.g., DBPII, MSP5, RBP2a, S16)
Designed and produced antigen haplotypes reflecting global sequence diversity, moving beyond the Sal-1 reference strain
Empirically validated haplotype performance using field sera from Brazil and Thailand cohorts
Demonstrated that antigen diversity impacts immunogenicity regionally but not always diagnostic accuracy — and that multi-antigen models can mitigate performance gaps
methods: Analysis of global P. vivax genetic sequence data to assess antigen variation
Design and production of antigen haplotypes representing prevalent circulating variants
Serological testing of antibody responses against haplotype vs. Sal-1 reference constructs
Correlation of antibody levels with timing of qPCR-detectable blood-stage P. vivax infection (within prior 9 months)
Classification model evaluation comparing single-antigen and multi-antigen strategies
experiments_or_evidence: Antibody response testing using samples from well-characterised cohorts in Brazil and Thailand
Assessment of antibody levels relative to time since qPCR-detectable P. vivax infection
Comparison of haplotype vs. Sal-1 constructs in identifying infection within prior 9 months
Evaluation of regional immunogenicity differences (e.g., RBP2a, DBPII) and their impact on classification accuracy
assumptions: Antibody levels correlate with recent P. vivax exposure and thus serve as proxies for hypnozoite carriage
qPCR-detectable blood-stage infection is a reliable temporal anchor for defining 'recent exposure'
Haplotype representation based on global sequence data captures immunologically relevant variation
limitations: Cohorts limited to Brazil and Thailand — generalisability to other endemic regions untested
Only 8 antigens examined; broader antigen panel not assessed
No direct hypnozoite detection — exposure inference remains indirect
Functional impact of haplotype variation on antibody binding affinity or epitope conformation not experimentally characterised

gap_signals.text: Cohorts limited to Brazil and Thailand — generalisability to other endemic regions untested
Functional impact of haplotype variation on antibody binding affinity or epitope conformation not experimentally characterised
No direct hypnozoite detection — exposure inference remains indirect
Only 8 antigens examined; broader antigen panel not assessed
Lack of field validation of antigen variants across diverse transmission settings before deployment.
Prior serological marker development relied predominantly on Sal-1 reference sequences without systematic assessment of global diversity impact.
Single-antigen assays may fail in high-diversity regions, yet multi-antigen strategies remain underexplored in serosurveillance design.

datasets_or_materials: Global P. vivax genetic sequence data
Serum samples from well-characterised cohorts in Brazil and Thailand
qPCR-confirmed P. vivax infection timing metadata

important_claims.claim: Extensive genetic diversity exists in key P. vivax antigens (e.g., DBPII, MSP5), with only 0.4% of global MSP5 sequences matching Sal-1.
important_claims.evidence: Analysis of global P. vivax genetic data showed sequence identity to Sal-1 ranging from 0.4% (MSP5) to 99% (S16).

important_claims.claim: Regional differences in immunogenicity (e.g., RBP2a, DBPII) affect serological assay performance.
important_claims.evidence: Two antigens exhibited strong differences in immunogenicity by region and construct.

important_claims.claim: Multi-antigen classification models can overcome performance deficits caused by antigen diversity.
important_claims.evidence: In cases where performance was affected (e.g., RBP2a), adding multiple antigens into the classification model restored accuracy.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: Provides a replicable pipeline — from diversity quantification to haplotype design to field validation — applicable to serological marker development for other genetically diverse pathogens.

keynote.title: Health Disparity and Geospatial Analysis among Cancer Patients in the US/Mexico Border Region of Imperial Valley
keynote.core_problem: Understanding spatial patterns and the association between travel distance to care and health outcomes (especially mortality) for cancer patients in an underserved, rural, minority-dominant US-Mexico border region (Imperial Valley).
contributions: Identification of three distinct spatial distribution patterns of cancers in Imperial Valley
Quantification of the association between ethnicity (Hispanic/Latino identity) and cancer mortality
Empirical assessment of travel distance–mortality relationship in a rural border setting
methods: Retrospective analysis of intake assessment data from a community-based cancer organization (2006–2022)
Geospatial visualization using ArcGIS Pro
Kernel Density Estimation (KDE) for spatial pattern mapping
Statistical modeling to assess associations between travel distance, ethnicity, and mortality
experiments_or_evidence: Odds of death found slightly higher among Hispanics/Latinos
Odds of mortality decreased as hospital distance increased (counterintuitive finding)
No statistically significant associations found between travel distance and cancer stage at diagnosis or mortality
Three spatial distribution patterns identified across cancer types
assumptions: Intake assessment data reliably captures patient demographics, residence, diagnosis, and outcomes
Travel distance (as measured) is a valid proxy for access to care
Mortality outcomes are accurately recorded and attributable within the dataset
limitations: Lack of statistical significance in travel distance–mortality association may reflect measurement error, confounding, or insufficient power
Retrospective, single-organization dataset limits generalizability
No adjustment reported for clinical variables (e.g., cancer type, stage, treatment received, comorbidities)
Temporal changes in care infrastructure or policy over 2006–2022 not accounted for

gap_signals.text: Lack of statistical significance in travel distance–mortality association may reflect measurement error, confounding, or insufficient power

Past research on travel and health outcomes exists but lacks focus on underserved rural border populations
Studies in rural regions such as the US-Mexico border are limited
Further investigation and better tools may yield clearer results regarding travel distance–outcome correlation

keynotes.title: Taurine is a Natural Host Cytoadhesion Inhibitor in Asymptomatic Malaria Cases

keynote.title: Taurine is a Natural Host Cytoadhesion Inhibitor in Asymptomatic Malaria Cases
keynote.core_problem: It is unclear whether host metabolic alterations contribute to asymptomatic malaria persistence during low-transmission dry seasons in sub-Saharan Africa, and how such alterations might modulate disease severity.
contributions: Identification of elevated plasma taurine as a metabolite signature associated with asymptomatic malaria during low transmission.
Demonstration that taurine inhibits cytoadhesion of Plasmodium falciparum-infected red blood cells (iRBCs) to CD36 and EPCR in vitro.
Proposal of a mechanistic link between taurine-mediated reduction in cytoadhesion and enhanced splenic clearance, contributing to asymptomatic infection and host resistance.
methods: Plasma metabolome profiling (n=199) across high and low malaria transmission periods in The Gambia.
Longitudinal sampling (n=16 individuals, monthly over six months) during low transmission season.
In vitro cytoadhesion assays measuring iRBC binding to CD36 and endothelial protein C receptor (EPCR).
Exogenous taurine treatment in adhesion assays to assess functional inhibition.
experiments_or_evidence: Metabolomic analysis revealing season- and pathogenicity-associated plasma metabolite changes.
Significant elevation of taurine specifically in asymptomatic infections during low transmission.
In vitro inhibition of iRBC binding to CD36 and EPCR by taurine.
Reduction or reversal of iRBC adhesion upon exogenous taurine addition.
assumptions: Cytoadhesion inhibition by taurine observed in vitro is physiologically relevant in human hosts.
Elevated plasma taurine levels causally contribute to asymptomatic status (rather than being merely correlative).
Enhanced splenic clearance is the primary downstream consequence of reduced cytoadhesion in this context.

gap_signals.text: No in vivo validation of taurine’s effect on cytoadhesion or disease outcome.
No direct evidence linking taurine levels to splenic clearance rates in humans.
Lack of clinical data on taurine supplementation efficacy or safety for malaria prophylaxis/therapy.

datasets_or_materials: Human plasma samples from n=199 individuals in The Gambia, spanning high and low malaria transmission periods.
Longitudinal plasma samples from n=16 individuals collected monthly over six months during low transmission season.

important_claims.claim: Elevated plasma taurine is associated with asymptomatic malaria during low transmission seasons.
important_claims.evidence: Metabolomic profiling of 199 plasma samples, including longitudinal sampling of 16 individuals, revealed significant taurine elevation specifically in asymptomatic cases during low transmission.

important_claims.claim: Taurine inhibits iRBC cytoadhesion to CD36 and EPCR in vitro.
important_claims.evidence: Exogenous taurine reduced or reversed iRBC binding to both CD36 and EPCR in controlled adhesion assays.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: Provides a biologically grounded, host-targeted candidate (taurine) and associated experimental paradigms (seasonal metabolomics + cytoadhesion assays) for studying host-directed interventions in malaria; dataset enables replication and integration with other omics studies of seasonal immunity.

keynote.core_problem: Impaired neural and behavioral entrainment to temporally variable rhythmic input in autism spectrum disorder (ASD), potentially underlying cognitive rigidity and atypical predictive processing.
contributions: Demonstrated reduced neural modulation by temporal regularity in ASD using high-density EEG
Showed diminished phase-behavior coupling under jittered rhythmic conditions in ASD
Ruled out evoked-response explanations via residual spectral power analysis
Identified correlation between neural modulation strength and IQ within the ASD group
methods: High-density EEG recording
Visual detection task with four parametrically varied rhythmic structures (from isochronous to highly irregular)
Spectral analysis
Temporal response function (TRF) modeling
Phase-behavior coupling analysis
Residual spectral power analysis post-stimulation

keynotes.title: Preventing premature deaths through polygenic risk scores

keynote.title: Preventing premature deaths through polygenic risk scores
keynote.core_problem: Quantifying the clinical utility of polygenic risk scores (PRS) for optimizing existing disease screening programs to prevent premature deaths.
contributions: Quantified the potential clinical benefits of PRS-guided screening across seven diseases with established screening programs.
Estimated optimal screening ages for individuals stratified by PRS-derived genetic risk (high, very high, reduced).
Reported case enrichment ratios for high-PRS groups across diseases.
Projected a 23.3% reduction in premature deaths among high-risk individuals via PRS-tailored screening.
methods: Integrated modelling approach combining PRS-based risk stratification and population-level screening guidelines.
Defined high genetic risk as PRS odds ratio >2, very high as >3, and reduced risk as <0.5.
Calculated optimal screening age based on equivalence to population-level risk at standard screening ages.
Leveraged published data on differential mortality and outcomes with/without screening interventions.
Computed case enrichment ratio as (percentage of cases in high-PRS group) / (percentage of cases in total population).
experiments_or_evidence: Empirical estimation of years earlier/later high/very high/reduced risk individuals reach equivalent population-level screening risk: 10.8 years earlier (very high), 8.9 years earlier (high), 16.8 years later (reduced).
Case enrichment in high-PRS group ranged from 1.7 to 3.0 across diseases.
Projected 23.3% reduction in premature deaths among high-risk individuals across all seven diseases.
assumptions: PRS-derived odds ratios reliably reflect lifetime genetic risk.
Population-level screening age recommendations are based on average population risk thresholds.
Screening interventions applied at PRS-optimized ages yield proportional mortality reductions as observed in published studies.
PRS remains stable and actionable from a single biological sample over lifetime.
limitations: Reliance on published data for mortality and intervention outcomes—not primary experimental validation.
No description of PRS construction methodology (e.g., training cohorts, SNP selection, clumping/thresholding).
No discussion of ancestry diversity or portability of PRS across populations.
No reporting of uncertainty intervals (e.g., confidence ranges) for projected benefits.

gap_signals.text: No description of PRS construction methodology (e.g., training cohorts, SNP selection, clumping/thresholding).
No discussion of ancestry diversity or portability of PRS across populations.
No reporting of uncertainty intervals (e.g., confidence ranges) for projected benefits.
Reliance on published data for mortality and intervention outcomes—not primary experimental validation.
No details provided on how PRS were built (e.g., base GWAS, LD pred method, ancestry composition), limiting reproducibility and generalizability.
Projections rely on modelling and published outcomes—not prospective or randomized evidence of PRS-guided screening efficacy.
No analysis of performance or benefit across ancestral or demographic subgroups.

important_claims.claim: Very high genetic risk individuals reach the risk level associated with usual screening age on average 10.8 years earlier than the general population.
important_claims.evidence: Integrated modelling using PRS odds ratios and population risk trajectories.

important_claims.claim: Case enrichment in high-PRS groups ranges from 1.7 to 3.0 across diseases.
important_claims.evidence: Calculated case enrichment ratio from disease-specific incidence and PRS-stratified prevalence.

important_claims.claim: PRS-guided screening could reduce premature deaths in high-risk individuals by 23.3% across all seven diseases.
important_claims.evidence: Modelling leveraging published mortality and intervention outcome data.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: Provides a generalizable framework for PRS-informed screening age optimization; enables benchmarking of PRS clinical utility across diseases; highlights need for standardized reporting of PRS construction and validation in clinical modelling contexts.

A comprehensive, multi-omics, single-cell pipeline for grading and validating safe and effective CAR targets in ovarian cancer, including a three-tiered homogeneity classification system and integrated computational-experimental framework.

Abstract: Chimeric antigen receptor (CAR) T-cell therapy has achieved remarkable success in hematologic malignancies but continues to face significant barriers in solid tumors such as ovarian cancer (OC), primarily due to antigen heterogeneity and on-target, off-tumor toxicity. Traditional discovery...[truncated]
Full text excerpt:
Traditional discovery methods that rely on bulk tissue analyses are inadequate to overcome these challenges.

We detail a sequential framework that integrates bulk omics atlases, single-cell and spatial transcriptomics, glycoproteomics, and immune phenotyping to deconstruct both tumor complexity and the immunosuppressive microenvironment.
We then outline a tiered experimental validation pathway, from patient-derived organoids (PDOs) to in vivo safety models.

The practical utility of this atlas-driven approach is demonstrated through a case study on claudin 18.2 (CLDN18.2), in which integrated data diagnosis informs rational engineering strategies such as combinatorial targeting and affinity tuning to mitigate limitations.

evidence_type_annotations.claim: omics profiling

A comprehensive, multi-omics, single-cell pipeline for grading and validating safe and effective CAR targets in ovarian cancer, including a three-tiered homogeneity classification system and integrated computational-experimental framework.

Abstract: Chimeric antigen receptor (CAR) T-cell therapy has achieved remarkable success in hematologic malignancies but continues to face significant barriers in solid tumors such as ovarian cancer (OC), primarily due to antigen heterogeneity and on-target, off-tumor toxicity. Traditional discovery...[truncated]
Full text excerpt:
Traditional discovery methods that rely on bulk tissue analyses are inadequate to overcome these challenges.

We detail a sequential framework that integrates bulk omics atlases, single-cell and spatial transcriptomics, glycoproteomics, and immune phenotyping to deconstruct both tumor complexity and the immunosuppressive microenvironment.
We then outline a tiered experimental validation pathway, from patient-derived organoids (PDOs) to in vivo safety models.

The practical utility of this atlas-driven approach is demonstrated through a case study on claudin 18.2 (CLDN18.2), in which integrated data diagnosis informs rational engineering strategies such as combinatorial targeting and affinity tuning to mitigate limitations.

Full text excerpt: A comprehensive, multi-omics, single-cell pipeline for grading and validating safe and effective CAR targets in ovarian cancer, including a three-tiered homogeneity classification system and integrated computational-experimental framework.
